migrate_modal.heading: Move your decisions into the macOS desktop app
migrate_modal.intro: The desktop build is required for one-click uninstalls — Apple Configurator only runs on macOS, and we use it to remove apps from a connected iPhone or iPad after a verified local backup.
migrate_modal.step_1_body: Grab the latest macOS build from the project releases page. The download is a .dmg signed by the same developer ID as the web app you're using now.
migrate_modal.step_2_body: We'll bundle every app, verdict, note, and shortlist into a portable .audit.json file. The export carries a small migration flag so the desktop app knows to drop you straight back into this Review wizard once the import lands — no re-onboarding.
migrate_modal.step_2_done: ✓ Bundle saved to your Downloads folder
migrate_modal.step_2_error: Couldn't build the bundle — check the AI / export gate in Settings or try again.
migrate_modal.step_3_body: Open the desktop app, choose Settings → Import audit bundle, and pick the file you just exported. The migration flag fires once on import and lands you on this same Review wizard so you can finish your decisions there.

print.replace_hint: Use privacytracker's Compare view to find safer alternatives.

risk.high_desc: Apps that track you across other apps and websites, or collect sensitive data.
risk.moderate_desc: Apps linking a meaningful amount of data to your identity.
risk.low_desc: Apps collecting a limited amount of identifiable data.
risk.minimal_desc: Apps collecting no linked or tracking data.
risk.unknown_desc: The developer hasn't filled in privacy labels yet — Apple will require them on the next update.

severity.data_not_linked_to_you_desc: Data the app collects but says is not tied back to your identity, usually because direct identifiers are removed before collection.
severity.data_linked_to_you_desc: Data the app can connect back to you, such as through your account, device, contact details, user ID, or another identifier.
severity.data_used_to_track_you_desc: Data the app or its partners use to recognize you across other companies' apps, websites, offline data sources, or data brokers.

data_label_hint.trigger_label: What does this mean? Show example

contact_info.data_used_to_track_you: Give one app your email, and a week later your inbox fills with brands you never signed up with. Your address got passed along — and it finds you by name.
contact_info.data_linked_to_you: Your name, email and phone run your account here — they sign you in, send your receipts, track your delivery. Tied to you, kept in this one app.
contact_info.data_not_linked_to_you: Your address is struck out before anything is stored. What's kept is a sign-up tally — domains and countries — with no addresses in it.

health_and_fitness.data_used_to_track_you: Your step count leaves the app and reaches an insurer's model. Low-activity weeks read as risk — and your premium gets recalculated upward.
health_and_fitness.data_linked_to_you: Your workouts are filed under your name here — weekly trends, streaks, rest days. The app knows your body's patterns and keeps them to itself.
health_and_fitness.data_not_linked_to_you: Your sample is stripped of identity and pooled with 50,000 others. The averages are real; none of them point back to you.

financial_info.data_used_to_track_you: Enter your salary and assets, and a booking site quotes you a higher airfare — same seat, same flight, priced for what they know you can pay.
financial_info.data_linked_to_you: Your card and balance are pinned to your account here — saved for checkout, under your name, visible to this app only.
financial_info.data_not_linked_to_you: Your amount drops into an anonymous total. The aggregate spend stat is real; your slice of it can't be traced back.

location.data_used_to_track_you: Your GPS trail pins your home address and your daily pattern — when you leave, when you're back. Enough to identify you without ever asking your name.
location.data_linked_to_you: Your home, your work, your morning commute get filed against your profile here. The app remembers your routine — and keeps it in-app.
location.data_not_linked_to_you: Your pings are pooled by area before storage — one dot among 48,210 in your region today, with no names and no device IDs.

sensitive_info.data_used_to_track_you: Nothing was asked. Your faith and politics were inferred from what you read and joined, packaged into an audience segment, and sold — 'Why this ad?' quietly names it.
sensitive_info.data_linked_to_you: The app holds its guess about your beliefs and uses it to tune what you see — inside this one app, not sold on.
sensitive_info.data_not_linked_to_you: Your identity is struck from the signal at source. What survives is a regional count — one of 64,000 — with no names attached.

contacts.data_used_to_track_you: Share your address book and the app builds a file on Sarah — her name, her number, who she knows — though she never signed up for anything.
contacts.data_linked_to_you: Your contacts are filed against your profile to power 'people you may know'. The app knows your circle; it stays in this app.
contacts.data_not_linked_to_you: Your contacts are reduced to a count before storage. '312 contacts' is all that survives — no names, no numbers.

user_content.data_used_to_track_you: Your photo library becomes training data. The model learns your face from your own pictures — and can now recognise it anywhere.
user_content.data_linked_to_you: Your photos are filed in your library here — backed up under your account so your devices stay in sync. Yours, in this one app.
user_content.data_not_linked_to_you: Owner and location are stripped from each image first. Your photo becomes one of 10 million in an anonymous pool.

browsing_history.data_used_to_track_you: The product page you looked at follows you. An ad for the same shoes turns up inside a completely unrelated app — matched to you.
browsing_history.data_linked_to_you: The pages you read are filed into 'interests' on your profile here, and your feed quietly reshapes around them.
browsing_history.data_not_linked_to_you: Your visit is counted as a topic tally — sport, travel, finance. The counts are real; who visited is stripped.

search_history.data_used_to_track_you: Search for pasta recipes here, and a chef's-knife ad meets you inside an unrelated news app. Your query travelled — and it was matched to you.
search_history.data_linked_to_you: Your queries stay on your profile here — type 'pa' and it already suggests your pasta searches. Convenient, and contained to this app.
search_history.data_not_linked_to_you: Your query is struck from the log and folded into trends. 'Pasta recipes is trending' is real; whose searches made it isn't kept.

identifiers.data_used_to_track_you: Your device's advertising ID is the same in every app. Three apps compare notes, the IDs line up — and three separate yous become one profile.
identifiers.data_linked_to_you: Your account ID ties your phone, tablet and laptop together — the same you across devices, inside this one service.
identifiers.data_not_linked_to_you: A throwaway session token stands in for your identity, then expires. The behaviour data can't be walked back to you.

purchases.data_used_to_track_you: Buy a dog bowl and puppy food, and an ad for puppy gear meets you inside an unrelated puzzle app. Your receipt told a story — and it was matched to you.
purchases.data_linked_to_you: Your orders are filed in your account — for receipts and easy reorder. They know what you bought; it isn't sold on.
purchases.data_not_linked_to_you: Your name comes off the receipt before storage. '$51 · pet store · today' joins the aggregate; who paid isn't kept.

usage_data.data_used_to_track_you: The app learns your weak moment — 11pm, every night. A notification lands on your lock screen at 11:04, timed to pull you back in.
usage_data.data_linked_to_you: Where you left off is kept on your profile so the app can drop you straight back in — 60% through the article. Used here, not sold.
usage_data.data_not_linked_to_you: Your user id is struck from each event. Your tap becomes +1 on a product-analytics funnel — open, browse, buy — with no user attached.

diagnostics.data_used_to_track_you: A crash report's boring facts — model, OS, battery — combine into a fingerprint. In a crowd of phones, exactly one matches: yours.
diagnostics.data_linked_to_you: Your crash is ticketed against your account, the bug gets reproduced, and the fix ships back to your device. Tied to you so they can fix your bug.
diagnostics.data_not_linked_to_you: The device id is struck before the report ships. Your crash becomes +1 on a dashboard — 0.3% of sessions — with no devices in it.

other.data_used_to_track_you: The label only says 'other'. Whatever it is, it's filed with everything else they hold on you — matched to you, contents undisclosed.
other.data_linked_to_you: Unnamed data sits on file with your account, used 'for app functionality' — which is all the label says. Kept in this one app.
other.data_not_linked_to_you: The user id is struck from each record at source. What's kept is a pile of misc records with no one attached — yours points at nobody.

ai_step.eyebrow: One more thing about each app
ai_step.lede: Apple's privacy labels only cover what the app developer tells Apple. They don't list every business behind that app — the email provider, identity broker, payment processor, analytics gateway, or other subprocessors that quietly handle your data once it leaves the app.
ai_step.lede_paragraph_2: The developer's privacy policy is usually the closest thing to a complete picture: it spells out who the data goes to and what happens once it gets there. We download a copy of each policy and watch for changes, so you'll know when one quietly shifts.
ai_step.ai_offer_heading: Would you like an AI to summarise the policies?
ai_step.subtitle: Privacy policies are long. With your permission, we can ask an AI to read each one and produce a short, plain-English summary across eight common lenses (data sharing, retention, sale of data, etc.) so you don't have to. Entirely optional — skip to the dashboard if you'd rather read them yourself, or come back later from Settings.
ai_step.hint_openai: Best default if you already have an OpenAI key.
ai_step.hint_custom: Use Ollama locally or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
ai_step.api_key_help_optional: Optional. Leave blank for local Ollama endpoints.

ai_disclosure_page.metadata_description: How privacytracker uses AI to summarise privacy policies and detect label changes.
ai_disclosure_page.title: AI disclosure
ai_disclosure_page.lede: privacytracker can optionally use AI models to help make sense of developer privacy policies. This page explains what AI is used for, what data is sent where, and how to turn it off.
ai_disclosure_page.section_used_for_p1: When enabled, privacytracker fetches each tracked app's developer privacy policy and asks a language model to produce a structured summary keyed to specific lenses — collection scope, advertising and marketing, third-party sharing, and similar topics. The raw policy text is hashed so we only re-summarise when the document actually changes, and long policies are split into ~12,000-character chunks so smaller local models can process them.
ai_disclosure_page.section_used_for_p2: All scraping, diffing, and notification logic is deterministic and runs locally in the Next.js server process. AI is used only for the natural-language summarisation step — it does not decide whether a label changed, and it cannot alter the stored privacy label data.
ai_disclosure_page.section_provider_intro: You choose the provider in Settings. Supported options are:
ai_disclosure_page.provider_disabled_text: — no AI calls are made. privacytracker still scrapes labels, diffs changes, and sends notifications normally.
ai_disclosure_page.provider_openai_text: — policy text is sent to OpenAI's API using the API key you provide.
ai_disclosure_page.provider_anthropic_text: — policy text is sent to Anthropic's API using the API key you provide.
ai_disclosure_page.provider_custom_text: — any OpenAI-compatible endpoint you configure, such as a local Ollama instance, Llama.cpp server, or a self-hosted gateway. Nothing leaves your machine unless the endpoint you point at forwards it.
ai_disclosure_page.section_provider_footer_pre: The full list of providers and their defaults lives in
ai_disclosure_page.section_provider_footer_post: . The project maintainers do not operate any hosted AI service on your behalf — API calls go directly from your server to the provider you configured.
ai_disclosure_page.section_data_p1: Only the text of the developer privacy policy itself is sent to the provider. App names, bundle IDs, your notification history, your settings, and the SQLite database contents are not sent. Policies are public documents published by the app's developer — privacytracker downloads them directly from the URL Apple links to on the App Store page.
ai_disclosure_page.section_data_p2: The provider's own terms govern what happens to that text once it reaches them (retention, logging, training). Review those terms for whichever provider you choose, especially if you plan to point the tool at internal or sensitive documents.
ai_disclosure_page.section_accuracy_p1: AI-generated summaries are a convenience. They can hallucinate, omit material clauses, or mischaracterise scope. Treat them as a starting point and always consult the original policy — which privacytracker links to from every app's detail page — before relying on a claim in a compliance, legal, or publishing context.
ai_disclosure_page.section_accuracy_p2: The privacy label data shown on app detail pages is scraped directly from the App Store and is not AI-generated. Label changes in the notification feed are detected by deterministic diffing of those scraped labels.
ai_disclosure_page.section_built_p1: privacytracker itself was built with substantial help from large language models. A mixture of frontier models was used across planning, implementation, refactoring, and review, including:
ai_disclosure_page.model_gemini: Google Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3 Flash
ai_disclosure_page.section_built_p2: Using several models in combination helped cross-check design decisions, catch bugs that any single model missed, and keep the code idiomatic across the Next.js, SQLite, and scraping layers. A human author reviewed and integrated every change — the models assisted, they did not ship code unsupervised.
ai_disclosure_page.section_built_p3: This is disclosed here in the interest of transparency: if you are auditing privacytracker's own behaviour, you should know that its source code was co-authored with AI tooling, even though the runtime privacy-label scraping and diffing logic is fully deterministic.
ai_disclosure_page.section_off_post_pre: and set the AI provider to
ai_disclosure_page.section_off_post_suffix: . Existing summaries remain in the database until you clear them, but no new AI requests will be made. You can also delete stored summaries per-app from the app detail view.
ai_disclosure_page.footer_pre: Questions or concerns? The source is open —

about_page.tagline: Watch every Apple App Store privacy label for the apps on your iPhone, summarise the developer's policy with a local or cloud LLM, and back-fill history from the Internet Archive — all on this Mac, no cloud account.
about_page.feature_labels_blurb: Track every privacy label Apple publishes for the apps on your iPhone, automatically diffed each time they change.
about_page.feature_policies_blurb: Local or cloud LLM reads each app's policy and surfaces what data is collected, shared, and sold.
about_page.feature_wayback_blurb: Back-fill privacy-label history from the Internet Archive so you can see what apps used to declare years ago.
about_page.feature_cfgutil_blurb: One-click import of every app installed on a connected iPhone via cfgutil — no typing names by hand.
about_page.feature_audit_blurb: Export and share a recommended-changes bundle with someone you're helping; their app folds it back in.
about_page.feature_notes_blurb: Keep freeform notes per app and mark each one Safe, Replace, or Uninstall to plan a privacy audit.
about_page.feature_local_blurb: Everything stays in a SQLite file on this Mac. No telemetry, no accounts, no cloud sync unless you wire it up.

help_export.metadata_description: How to export a list of installed iPhone apps from a computer for import into privacytracker.
help_export.title: Export your iPhone app list as a file
help_export.subtitle: If your app list is already on a computer, the easiest path is to generate a small `.csv` or `.txt` export and upload it back into privacytracker.
help_export.mac_section_copy: Use Finder to create a local backup first, then run the helper script against that backup.
help_export.mac_step_1: Connect your iPhone to your Mac and trust the computer if prompted.
help_export.mac_step_2: Open Finder, select your iPhone, and create a local backup.
help_export.mac_step_3: Run the helper script below from this project folder.
help_export.mac_step_4: Upload the generated `.csv` or `.txt` file in onboarding.
help_export.windows_section_copy: Use Apple Devices or iTunes to create a local backup, then export the app list from that backup.
help_export.windows_step_1: Connect your iPhone and trust the computer.
help_export.windows_step_2: Create a local backup using Apple Devices or iTunes.
help_export.windows_step_3: Run the same helper script after the backup finishes.
help_export.windows_step_4: Upload the exported file in privacytracker.
help_export.helper_section_copy: This project includes a helper that reads the newest local backup automatically and writes a `.txt`, `.csv`, and `.json` export.
help_export.device_section_copy: If you are on Mac, Linux, or Windows and have `ideviceinstaller` available, you can export directly from a connected phone instead of a backup.
help_export.upload_section_title: What to upload back into the app
help_export.upload_section_copy: After the helper runs, choose either the generated `.csv` or `.txt` file in onboarding. privacytracker reads the app names, then lets you confirm the App Store matches before importing.

live_text_modal.title: Copy app names from a screenshot
live_text_modal.copy: Your iPhone can read the text in any screenshot offline — no upload, no OCR engine to load in the browser. Do this once and paste the result into the box on the previous screen.
live_text_modal.step_1: Open the Photos app and tap a screenshot of your app list (Settings → General → iPhone Storage works well, or the App Library A–Z list).
live_text_modal.step_2: Tap the Live Text icon in the bottom-right corner of the photo — it looks like three lines inside a small frame.
live_text_modal.step_3: Tap and hold a word, then drag to highlight the whole list. Tap Select All if the app names fill the screen.
live_text_modal.step_4: Tap Copy, come back to this browser tab, and paste into the Type app names textarea.
live_text_modal.tip: Tip: apps hidden inside Home Screen folders won't be picked up by Live Text either — capture a flat list from iPhone Storage or the App Library for the full set.

manual_apps_banner.body: Track Safari web apps, TestFlight betas, personal Xcode builds, and sideloaded apps alongside the rest. They don't have App Store privacy labels, so you can record the privacy policy and notes manually.

risk_tier_legend.hint: Four tiers, derived from Apple's privacy labels. Tap to expand.
risk_tier_legend.intro: Each app carries a single risk pill based on what Apple's privacy labels say — not on what the app just changed. Accessibility and privacy-policy updates don't move the pill.
risk_tier_legend.footer: Labels are self-declared by developers, so the pill is a floor, not a ceiling — an app could collect more than it admits. Dive into the app's Privacy Labels tab to see exactly which categories and how they're used.

risk_tiers.high_rule: At least one data type declared as "Data Used to Track You".
risk_tiers.high_meaning: The developer reports that the app or its partners can use this data to recognize you outside the app — usually for advertising, measurement, retargeting, or data brokers.
risk_tiers.high_example: Typical of large social networks and ad-supported free apps that share identifiers with data brokers.
risk_tiers.moderate_rule: No cross-app tracking, but three or more data types linked to your identity.
risk_tiers.moderate_meaning: The app ties a lot of data to your account. It stays inside the app, but the developer still holds a rich profile of you.
risk_tiers.moderate_example: Typical of banking, shopping, streaming and communication apps where a lot is tied to your sign-in.
risk_tiers.low_rule: Some data collected, but only a small amount is linked to your identity.
risk_tiers.low_meaning: The app collects something — often diagnostics, optional usage stats, or a single linked category — without building a full profile.
risk_tiers.low_example: Typical of light-touch utilities, calculators, or reference apps that collect a crash log or optional analytics.
risk_tiers.minimal_rule: The developer declares no data collection at all.
risk_tiers.minimal_meaning: Apple's privacy labels show an empty sheet. Nothing the app says it collects, linked or otherwise.
risk_tiers.minimal_example: Typical of single-player offline games, simple reference tools, and some privacy-focused utilities.

privacy_policy_page.title: We don't collect your data.
privacy_policy_page.subtitle: privacytracker runs on your machine. There's no server-side account system, no analytics pipeline, no data broker in the loop. Because "we don't collect anything" is cheap to say, this page also lists every third-party endpoint the app may contact while you use it — so the full shape of what actually goes over the wire is visible.

disclosure.aria: Privacy disclosure: data not collected. The developer does not collect any data from this app.
disclosure.title: Data Not Collected
disclosure.body: The developer does not collect any data from this app.

sections.third_parties_full: Third parties the app talks to

bodies.nothing_lede: Nothing. No account, no email, no device identifier, no analytics ping, no crash report, no IP log.
bodies.nothing_body: The apps you add, the privacy-label history we retain for them, any privacy profile you configure, and any AI provider settings you save — all of it lives in a single SQLite file on the machine running privacytracker (./data/privacy.db by default). If you delete that file, the state is gone. We don't have a copy.
bodies.third_parties_lede: When you use privacytracker, it calls a small number of public endpoints on your behalf. Nothing here sends your personal identifiers — but these hosts see standard HTTP request metadata (your IP, your User-Agent, the URL you're fetching), so we list them all.
bodies.third_parties_body: Nothing in the browser talks to a third party — React, Next.js, ECharts, Tesseract, and the Inter typeface (v4.1, SIL OFL-1.1) all ship from the same origin as the app. The list below is everything the server fetches on your behalf.
bodies.appstore_sub: Required for the core loop. Without these the app can't find, identify, or sync iOS apps.
bodies.developer_sub: Directly from whichever host the developer publishes on. We don't route this through any intermediary.
bodies.archives_sub: Only contacted if you explicitly run a Wayback import from Settings → Wayback import.
bodies.ai_sub_prefix: Not contacted unless you turn on AI summaries in
bodies.ai_sub_suffix: and pick a provider. The default is "disabled".
bodies.update_check_sub: Polled at most once every 24 hours to see whether a newer privacytracker release is available. Sends nothing about you or your data — just a standard HTTP GET. Switch off in Settings → Updates if you'd rather check manually.
bodies.going_offline_p1: The front-end already makes zero third-party connections — Inter is self-hosted from /public/fonts/ under its original SIL OFL-1.1 licence, and every JS / CSS asset is served from the same origin. Nothing in the browser phones home to a CDN.
bodies.going_offline_p2: Server-side, you can cut the last two optional outbound calls from inside the app:
bodies.going_offline_step_ai: AI summaries — open Settings → AI Policy Summaries and set the provider to "Disabled", or point it at a local Ollama endpoint to keep summarisation on-device. The card flashes when you arrive so it's easy to find.
bodies.going_offline_step_wayback: Wayback imports — leave them alone. They're opt-in, so never running them keeps the Internet Archive out of the picture entirely.
bodies.going_offline_p3: That leaves only Apple's own endpoints (iTunes Search + App Store pages) and the developer privacy-policy links you explicitly scrape — both of which are the point of the app.
bodies.alternatives_p1: privacytracker's AI summaries are one way to make sense of long policy documents, but they're opt-in and narrow by design — we only summarise policies for apps you've actively added. Two long-running third-party projects tackle the same problem across a much wider catalogue:
bodies.alternatives_tosdr: Terms of Service; Didn't Read (ToSDR) ↗ — community-reviewed grades for thousands of services, each broken down into plain-language "points" (good / neutral / blocker) with links back to the clause they came from.
bodies.alternatives_privacyspy: PrivacySpy ↗ — open-source, machine-readable scoring of privacy policies across a curated list of services, with rubric-backed reasons for every rating.
bodies.alternatives_p2: privacytracker has no affiliation with either project — they're listed because readers ask about them and they're a useful complement to what this app does. If you decide to use either service, read their terms of service and privacy policy first — opening those sites means that host, not privacytracker, sees your request, and each project has its own funding model, data practices, and scope. A quick look at their "About" / "Privacy" pages on tosdr.org ↗ and privacyspy.org ↗ is the usual starting point.
bodies.questions_p1: If something on this page looks wrong, incomplete, or has drifted from what the app actually does, open an issue on GitHub. The link below pre-fills the issue body with a note that it came from /privacy-policy so the maintainer can see the context at a glance.

help_definitions_page.apple_accessibility_aria: Apple Accessibility Nutrition Labels documentation (opens in new tab)
help_definitions_page.title: App Store Privacy Label Definitions
help_definitions_page.subtitle: A plain-language summary of the privacy terms Apple uses on every App Store listing. All information is drawn from Apple's own documentation — deep links are included under each section so you can verify the source.

sections.how_developers_intro: Every app's privacy label groups the data it collects into three buckets, based on how the data is handled rather than what the data is. Apple summarises these as:
sections.data_categories: Data type categories developers may collect

severity_bodies.unlinked: Data the app collects but says is not tied back to your identity. To fit this bucket, direct identifiers should be removed before collection, and the developer should not try to reconnect the data to you later.
severity_bodies.linked: Data the app can connect back to you, such as through your account, device, contact details, user ID, or another identifier. This does not automatically mean the data is sold or used for ads; it may be used for app features, support, personalization, analytics, purchases, or security.
severity_bodies.track: Data the app or its partners use to recognize you outside this app. Apple uses this bucket when app data is linked with data from other companies' apps, websites, or offline sources for targeted ads or ad measurement, or when app data is shared with a data broker. This is the highest-severity bucket because it can follow activity across services.

help_definitions_page.labels_support_link: About privacy information on the App Store and the app's product page ↗
help_definitions_page.labels_support_lead: Apple maintains the full definitions on its support site:
help_definitions_page.categories_intro: Within each of the three buckets above, developers must declare the specific categories of data that are collected. The App Store uses the categories below; hover the information icon on any app detail page for a short description.
help_definitions_page.categories_outro_lead: Apple documents each category and how developers can use it on the
help_definitions_page.categories_outro_mid: . For a more accessible walk-through, see Apple's story
help_definitions_page.apple_apps_body: Some apps on your device — Messages, Maps, Safari, Mail, Photos, Health, Wallet, and so on — are developed by Apple itself. Their privacy labels are published on a dedicated page alongside the associated feature-level privacy explainers.
help_definitions_page.apple_apps_link: Apple's privacy labels for its own apps ↗
help_definitions_page.transparency_intro: Apple publishes a semi-annual Transparency Report disclosing government and private-party requests for customer data, account preservation, account restriction, and device identifier queries. The report is broken down country-by-country.
help_definitions_page.transparency_country_outro: — requests Apple received from government and private-party agencies in {country}.
help_definitions_page.transparency_global_outro: — Apple does not publish a dedicated {country} page; the global index lists every country report Apple discloses.
help_definitions_page.sources_intro: privacytracker surfaces the labels the developer declared, but the definitions themselves come from Apple. For the source of truth, see:

sources.labels_support_link: About privacy information on the App Store (support.apple.com) ↗
sources.labels_support_outro: — official definitions of “Data Not Linked to You”, “Data Linked to You”, and “Data Used to Track You”.
sources.developer_details_outro: — the developer-facing reference: every data type, every purpose, and what must be disclosed.
sources.labels_overview_outro: — labels for Apple's built-in apps (Maps, Messages, Safari, Health, …).
sources.story_outro: — Apple's plain-language overview for end users.
sources.transparency_outro: — semi-annual disclosure of government and private-party requests for user data.

category_descriptions.contact_info: Information such as name, email address, phone number, physical address, or other contact information.
category_descriptions.health_fitness: Data related to health, medical records, fitness routines, and exercise data.
category_descriptions.financial_info: Payment information (e.g., credit card number), credit information, or other financial details like salary, income, assets, or debts.
category_descriptions.location: Precise location (e.g., latitude/longitude) or approximate location data.
category_descriptions.sensitive_info: Data such as racial or ethnic data, sexual orientation, pregnancy or childbirth information, disability, religious or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, political opinion, genetic information, or biometric data.
category_descriptions.contacts: Information accessed from your address book, friend lists, or social graph.
category_descriptions.user_content: Photos, videos, audio recordings, emails, messages, or other content created or provided by the user within the app.
category_descriptions.browsing_history: Information about content you have viewed that is not part of the app itself, such as websites visited.
category_descriptions.search_history: Information about searches performed within the app.
category_descriptions.identifiers: User or account-level IDs (e.g., account ID, user ID, customer number) and device-level IDs (e.g., advertising identifier).
category_descriptions.purchases: Records of purchases or transaction history.
category_descriptions.usage_data: Information about how you interact with the app, such as feature usage, product interaction, or performance metrics.
category_descriptions.diagnostics: Technical data like crash logs, launch time, hang rate, energy use, or other information used to measure app performance.
category_descriptions.other: Any data that does not fit into the other specific categories.

help_focus_page.title: Your focus
help_focus_page.subtitle: privacytracker tailors what you see based on two simple questions: who you're auditing apps for, and what you want from the app. Together they set sensible defaults across the dashboard, app detail pages, settings, and onboarding.

legal_page.title: Open-source libraries & licences
legal_page.subtitle: privacytracker is built on open-source software. This page lists every third-party library bundled with the app, grouped by SPDX licence identifier, with a short description, how we use it, and links to the source. The app itself is released under the Apache 2.0 licence — see the LICENSE file in the repository.
legal_page.audit_bundle_note: Audit bundles. If you export an audit bundle from Settings → Export Data, the resulting file contains your tracked apps, public privacy labels, change history, and any non-private annotations you've made. Annotations marked private never travel — the SQL filter in lib/audit-bundle.ts excludes them at the source, with no force-include path. Bundles do not include your AI provider keys, your sync schedule preferences, or any device identifiers. Treat exported bundles like any sensitive data file: encrypt at rest, share over secure channels.

subtitles.desktop_app: Tauri-only settings (auto-launch, dock badge, system-tray icon, native notifications) live here in the v0.1.0 desktop wrapper. The flag is wired so an explicit `on` override surfaces this placeholder when previewing the layout outside the desktop build.
subtitles.privacy_profile: For each category, pick the most invasive use you'll tolerate. Tracked apps that go further are flagged as mismatches on their card and in “Consider replacing”.
subtitles.notifications: Choose which events the notification bell shows in the top nav. Turning a type off hides existing entries of that type too — flipping it back on brings them straight back, nothing is lost.
subtitles.sync_schedule: The app will automatically re-scrape all your tracked apps on this interval to detect privacy label changes.
subtitles.app_store_region: The storefront we use when matching app names against the App Store. Regional listings can differ — switch to your country for more accurate matches on local banking, streaming, or government apps.
subtitles.accessibility_labels: Track Apple's accessibility nutrition labels — the shelf on each App Store listing that declares which features the developer says they support (VoiceOver, Voice Control, Larger Text, Captions, and more). Changes are flagged on the same timeline as privacy-label diffs.
subtitles.review_queue: Display preferences for the Tinder-style verdict carousel on the Apps page.
subtitles.accessibility_profile: For each accessibility feature, pick whether you need it, it's nice to have, or doesn't matter. Apps that don't declare features you asked for are flagged as mismatches, with required features counting twice as heavily as nice-to-haves.
subtitles.app_store_sync_status: Manually trigger an App Store label sync or check when the next automatic sync is scheduled. This refreshes the data categories Apple lists on each app's store page — it does NOT re-fetch developer privacy policies. To refresh policies in bulk see “Privacy Policies” below.
subtitles.ai_summaries: Choose the AI backend used to summarise developer privacy policies. After saving, re-sync an app to generate or refresh its summary.
subtitles.privacy_policies: Fetch and (optionally) re-summarise the developer privacy policy for every app that exposes one. Runs sequentially to stay gentle on publishers and your AI provider. You can navigate away while it's working — progress stays live in the background-task tray.
subtitles.policy_change_alerts: When a rescrape detects that a privacy policy's text has changed, the AI Policy tab shows a banner linking to the line-by-line diff on the History tab. Pick how long the banner should keep showing after the change is captured.
subtitles.policy_scrape_throttle: Skip repeat privacy-policy scrapes within a cooldown window. Applies to manual Re-sync, scheduled background runs, and import flows — each app is rate-limited individually, not globally. Disable this to force every Re-sync click to hit the network (useful when testing).
subtitles.policy_scrape_disabled: Stop all background privacy-policy fetching — including the per-app auto-fetch that fires after every App Store sync, the bulk runner, and resumed-after-crash sync queues. A safer dial than the throttle when you don't want the app reaching out to publishers at all.
subtitles.import_history: Every batch of apps you added during onboarding lives here. Review what each app matched with, fix a wrong match, or roll a whole import back.
subtitles.deployment_diagnostics: Check whether this install can serve requests, write to its SQLite database, and see the proxy headers a LAN deployment should send.
subtitles.backup_restore: Snapshot your entire database to a portable JSON file, or restore a previous backup. Your data stays on your machine.
subtitles.export_data: Download all tracked privacy data for backup, analysis, or sharing.
subtitles.reset_app: Delete all tracked apps, privacy snapshots, notifications, sync history, and saved settings, then return to onboarding.
subtitles.developer_options: Capture the exact prompts and responses the app sends to your AI provider, for troubleshooting only. Kept on the server in a small rolling window — never sent anywhere else.

notification_prefs_card.autosave_log_label: Also log settings auto-saves to the Task Center
notification_prefs_card.autosave_log_help: Each successful or failed settings save adds a one-line entry to the Task Center dropdown. Off by default — most people just want the bottom-of-screen toast. Stored per-browser.

sync_status.sync_now: ↻ Sync All App Store Pages Now

deployment_diagnostics_card.admin_unlock_body_configured: Enter the shared admin token once per browser session. It is kept in sessionStorage and attached only to same-origin destructive API calls.
deployment_diagnostics_card.admin_unlock_body_off: No admin token is configured on the server. For LAN browser access, prefer proxy-level access control.
deployment_diagnostics_card.admin_unlock_saved: Admin token saved for this browser session.
deployment_diagnostics_card.admin_unlock_failed: Could not save the admin token in this browser session.
deployment_diagnostics_card.copy_failed: Could not copy the support bundle.
deployment_diagnostics_card.unavailable_body: The diagnostics endpoint did not respond. Check the server log for details.
deployment_diagnostics_card.locked_body: You're connecting from a non-local address, so deployment diagnostics (and other sensitive data) stay hidden until you log in with the admin token.
deployment_diagnostics_card.locked_body_no_token: You're connecting from a non-local address and this server has no admin token configured, so this data can't be unlocked. Start the server with AUDITOR_ADMIN_TOKEN set, or browse from the machine itself (localhost).

privacy_policies_card.rescrape_title: Re-download each developer privacy policy. AI summaries are only regenerated when the text has actually changed.
privacy_policies_card.summarise_title: Re-download each policy and re-run the AI summary for every app, even if the text is unchanged. Uses AI credits.
privacy_policies_card.force_label: Force re-scrape (bypass the throttle for this batch)
privacy_policies_card.force_help: By default, bulk scraping respects the per-app cooldown set in “Policy Scrape Throttle” above so a recently-fetched app isn't hit again. Tick this to override the throttle for this batch only — the global setting stays unchanged.

backup_card.download_copy: Exports every tracked app, privacy snapshot, policy summary, import record, notification, and setting as a single versioned JSON file.
backup_card.restore_copy_after: current data with the contents of a backup file. You'll see a preview and have to confirm before anything is overwritten.
backup_card.wait_for_sync: Wait for the current sync to finish before restoring.
backup_card.download_current_before_restore: Before replacing this database, download a fresh backup of the current state.
backup_card.download_before_reset: Before deleting this database, download a fresh backup of the current state.
backup_card.destructive_hint: Restore is destructive — it wipes the current database inside a transaction, then replays the rows from the backup. Keep the exported file somewhere safe.
backup_card.snapshots_copy: Keep rolling JSON backups on the machine running privacytracker. These stay under the app data folder and are pruned automatically.
backup_card.snapshots_enabled_help: The server checks on startup and every 30 minutes, then creates a snapshot when this interval is due.
backup_card.snapshots_next_due: Due on the next server check

provider.title: 1 · Provider
provider.desc: Pick who summarises the policy text. Everything else adapts to what you choose here.

connection.title: 2 · Connection
connection.desc_with_key: Where to send requests and which key to use
connection.base_url_help_anthropic: Use the Anthropic API root. The app will call the Messages API under /v1/messages.
connection.base_url_help_custom: Use an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. For Ollama, enter the host and port and the app will add /v1 automatically if needed.
connection.base_url_help_default: Use the default OpenAI endpoint unless you are targeting an OpenAI-compatible gateway.
connection.api_key_help: Stored locally in this app database and used only for privacy-policy summary requests.
connection.custom_key_label: My endpoint requires an API key
connection.custom_key_help: Most local setups (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio) don't. Tick this only for gateways like OpenRouter, Together, or a corporate proxy that authenticate with a bearer token.

model.title: 3 · Model
model.desc_custom: Pick a model your endpoint already exposes, or choose “Custom model…” to type the ID of one you'll pull/serve.
model.desc_default: Choose a preset, or pick “Custom” to enter your own model name.
model.label: Model
model.refresh_title: Re-check which models your endpoint exposes
model.error_after: Pick “Custom model…” below and enter a model ID manually.
model.found_count: {count, plural, one {Found # model on your endpoint.} other {Found # models on your endpoint.}}
model.found_custom_extra: Enter a custom model name below.
model.no_models: Your endpoint didn't report any models. Pull one first (e.g. {code}) or pick “Custom model…” and type the ID manually.
model.help_custom_input: Enter the exact model name exposed by your provider below.
model.help_scanning: Scanning your endpoint for available models…
model.custom_id_help: Must match a model string your provider accepts.

sample.title: 4 · Model quality trial
sample.desc: Use a fictional policy with known facts to judge whether this model can produce a useful privacy summary.
sample.frame_aria: How to interpret the sample policy test
sample.policy_body: A made-up notes app policy with deliberately clear clauses about collection, ads, sharing, analytics, controls, retention, and children.
sample.judging_body: The selected model and its summary output. The sample policy is only the test fixture, not a recommendation or real app assessment.
sample.look_for_body: Good output should catch the known facts below, stay grounded in the policy, and avoid vague or unsupported claims.
sample.disabled_help: Choose a provider, base URL, API key if required, and model first.
sample.review_note_label: Use this result to compare models
sample.lenses_help: Each lens summary should name a concrete practice from the sample policy. Generic text or unsupported ratings are signs this model may be weak for policy summaries.
sample.empty_help: Run the trial when you want to compare models. The result should make it obvious whether the model extracts the important clauses or drifts into generic advice.

behavior.desc: Control when the app runs summaries.
behavior.summarize_on_import_help_disabled: Pick an AI provider above to enable this.
behavior.summarize_on_import_help_active: If on, onboarding waits for policy summaries. Turn off to get apps into the dashboard faster and summarise on demand later.

ollama_help.summary_copy: Quick-start: run your own free model locally with Ollama.
ollama_help.intro: Install Ollama, pull a model, then start the server. After that, pick <own>Own Model</own> as the backend above and leave the default base URL in the next step.
ollama_help.install_comment_2: # 2. Pull a small, capable model (~4 GB)
ollama_help.long_policies_body: Raise the context window so the model sees the whole document in one pass. Ollama's default is 2 K tokens; policies routinely need 32 K+. Export before starting the server:
ollama_help.memory_32k: <c>32K</c> context → <s>8 GB</s> unified RAM (Apple Silicon) or <s>8 GB</s> GPU VRAM.
ollama_help.memory_128k: <c>128K</c> context → <s>16 GB</s> unified RAM (Apple Silicon) or <s>16 GB</s> GPU VRAM.
ollama_help.memory_followup: Bigger models or larger contexts will need proportionally more memory — if Ollama starts falling back to CPU, drop the context length or pick a smaller quant.
ollama_help.verify: Verify everything is reachable with the Test connection button in the Connection step once you've entered the base URL.

ai_debug.label: Record AI prompts and responses
ai_debug.help: When on, each request to your AI provider (plus its response or error) is mirrored to the server console and captured below. Save AI settings to apply.
ai_debug.empty_active: Nothing captured yet. Trigger an AI call (e.g. summarise a policy) and refresh.
ai_debug.empty_off: Logging is currently off. Turn it on above and save AI settings to start capturing calls.

activity_log.retention_body: activity-log retention is uncapped in v1. Configurable retention (days) lands in v0.1.0 alongside the prune job.
activity_log.summary: Activity log · recent scrapes, re-syncs & summaries
activity_log.help: A rolling timeline of what the app has been doing in the background — scrapes, re-syncs, policy fetches and AI summaries, scheduled runs, and backup/restore. Handy for spotting failures or confirming that auto-sync is actually running. The last 2,000 events are kept.
activity_log.live_title_paused: Live updates paused — click to resume
activity_log.live_title_active: Auto-refreshing every 3 seconds — click to pause
activity_log.empty_filter: No {filter} events match the current filters.
activity_log.empty_default: No activity recorded yet. Trigger a re-sync or let the scheduled sync run, then refresh.

ai_timeouts.help: How long to wait for each AI call before giving up. Leave blank to use the provider-appropriate default (90s hosted, 3–6 min local). Raise the merge value first if you see a timeout notification in the bell — the merge call has to emit the full summary schema and on local models regularly needs several minutes. Min 10s, max 15 min.
ai_timeouts.direct_help: Used when the policy fits in one call.
ai_timeouts.chunk_help: Per chunk when the policy is split into several pieces.
ai_timeouts.merge_help: Final step that emits the full lens summary. Usually the slowest.
ai_timeouts.footer: Changes are persisted with the rest of the AI settings — scroll back up and click <save>Save AI Settings</save> to apply.

presets.body: a quick-pick row of canned focus profiles ("Self · understand", "Loved one · declutter", "Guardian · minimal") will appear here once the apply-preset logic ships. The flag is wired so this strip previews under an explicit `on` override.

wayback.title: Historical Import (Wayback Machine)
wayback.subtitle: Back-fill privacy-label history from archive.org, one snapshot per calendar quarter since the App Store web launch on 5 November 2025. If a capture for a specific month isn't available, the closest one is used instead. Nothing outside archive.org is contacted.
wayback.import_title: Fetch each app's archived App Store page from the Wayback Machine and back-date privacy-label snapshots into the changelog.
wayback.pause_title: Pause after the current app finishes. The queue stays saved and can be resumed later.
wayback.resume_title: Continue the paused Wayback import queue.
wayback.cancel_title: Immediately stop the active Wayback request and clear the saved queue. Imported rows that already landed are kept.
wayback.force_title: Discard the paused queue and start a fresh Wayback import across all apps.
wayback.remove_title: Delete every Wayback-imported snapshot. Live snapshots are kept.
wayback.show_imported_help: When on, back-dated rows appear in each app's Changelog with a clock icon and a purple “Wayback” label. Turning this off keeps the rows in the database but hides them from timelines — useful for quickly comparing the live-only view against the reconstructed one.
wayback.resume_body: The previous bulk import was interrupted; it's continuing automatically in the background.
wayback.paused_body: The saved queue will stay here until you resume it, cancel it, or start over from scratch.
wayback.toast_pause_requested: Wayback import will pause after the current app.

remove_modal.title: Remove all imported history?
remove_modal.body: This removes every Wayback-imported history row across all apps. Live privacy snapshots are kept — only the back-dated archive imports are deleted. You can re-run “Import history for all apps” afterward to bring them back.

reset_app_card.wait_for_sync: Wait for the current sync to finish before resetting the app.
reset_app_card.modal_body_step_1: This will remove all apps you are tracking and clear your saved history. You will go back to the onboarding flow afterward.
reset_app_card.modal_body_step_2: A full reset permanently deletes tracked apps, privacy snapshots, notifications, and settings from this installation.

start_over.step_1_prompt: This wipes every app, note, and setting. The schema stays. Continue?
start_over.step_2_prompt: Are you sure? This cannot be undone.
start_over.backup_hint: Take a fresh backup first so this install can be recovered if you change your mind.

toasts.no_url_for_retry: ❌ No App Store URL to retry — use Change match instead.
toasts.no_matching_to_retry: Nothing to retry — no matching items have an App Store URL.
toasts.sync_done: {changes, plural, one {✓ Synced {synced} apps — # change detected} other {✓ Synced {synced} apps — # changes detected}}

profile_editor.clear_all_title: Clear all — remove every preference (no categories will be compared).
profile_editor.no_pref_title: Don't compare this category — it won't contribute to mismatches.
profile_editor.footer_empty: No preferences set. Your profile is empty and no apps will be flagged as a mismatch.
profile_editor.footer_with_set: {set, plural, one {# of {total} category has a preference. The remaining will not contribute to mismatches.} other {# of {total} categories have a preference. The remaining will not contribute to mismatches.}}

presets.section_hint: Pick a baseline, then fine-tune any row below.
presets.confirm_text: Replace your current preferences with the {preset} preset?

descriptions.strict: Restrict identity-linked data wherever possible. Many mainstream apps will mismatch.
descriptions.balanced: A sensible default. Strict on health, sensitive, and contacts; lenient on usage and diagnostics.
descriptions.anti_tracking: Only flag apps that share data with third-party trackers. Everything else is fine.
descriptions.permissive: Accept almost anything, but still flag tracking on health, financial, location, and sensitive data.

a11y_profile_editor.clear_all_title: Clear all — remove every preference (no features will be compared).
a11y_profile_editor.no_pref_title: Don't compare this feature — it won't contribute to mismatches.
a11y_profile_editor.footer_empty: No preferences set. Your accessibility profile is empty and no apps will be flagged as a mismatch.
a11y_profile_editor.footer_with_set: {set, plural, one {# of {total} feature has a preference. The remaining will not contribute to mismatches.} other {# of {total} features have a preference. The remaining will not contribute to mismatches.}}

desktop_app_card.heading: Desktop app
desktop_app_card.subhead: These settings only apply when privacytracker is running as a desktop app.
desktop_app_card.autostart_help: Launch privacytracker automatically when you sign in.
desktop_app_card.launch_hidden_label: Launch hidden in the menu bar
desktop_app_card.launch_hidden_help: Skip showing the window on boot — open it from the tray when you want it. Takes effect on next launch.
desktop_app_card.hide_dock_help: macOS only. The app keeps running in the menu bar; background sync continues as long as you don't choose Quit.
desktop_app_card.native_notifs_help: Route "new privacy changes detected" through the macOS Notification Center / Windows Action Center as well as the in-app bell. The Dock badge count is always on.
desktop_app_card.shortcut_help_pre: System-wide hotkey that brings privacytracker to the front. Use Tauri's accelerator syntax — e.g.
desktop_app_card.url_scheme_pre: You can also open links in the format
desktop_app_card.url_scheme_post: to jump directly to an app's detail page.
desktop_app_card.require_unlock_label: Require Touch ID / password to open the window
desktop_app_card.require_unlock_help: macOS only. Background sync still runs; only the window is gated. Touch ID is never used to protect backup files — those remain portable between the web and desktop builds.
desktop_app_card.auto_lock_help_pre: Re-lock the window after this many minutes of inactivity.
desktop_app_card.diagnostics_help_post: and related files. Logs live in a separate folder so backing up your data doesn't sweep up diagnostic breadcrumbs.
desktop_app_card.status_save_failed_check: Failed to save — check the activity log
desktop_app_card.status_autostart_blocked_on: Couldn't enable start-at-login (macOS may have blocked it — check System Settings → General → Login Items)
desktop_app_card.status_native_notifs_on: Native notifications will take effect on next launch
desktop_app_card.status_touchid_cancelled_unlock_off: Touch ID cancelled — unlock stays off
desktop_app_card.status_touchid_unavailable_unlock_off: Touch ID unavailable on this host — unlock stays off
desktop_app_card.status_shortcut_unbound: Shortcut saved but couldn't be bound
desktop_app_card.status_touchid_unavailable: Touch ID unavailable on this host
desktop_app_card.touchid_reason_enable: enable Touch ID unlock for privacytracker

import_history_card.subtitle: Every batch of apps you added during onboarding lives here. Review what each app matched with, fix a wrong match, or roll a whole import back.
import_history_card.queue_note: {count, plural, one {# app waiting for background import — open Import History to retry or review.} other {# apps waiting for background import — open Import History to retry or review.}}

audit_bundle_import.title: Import audit bundle
audit_bundle_import.subtitle_post: someone shared with you? Drop it here. Their notes attach to each app alongside any of your own.
audit_bundle_import.dropzone_aria: Drop your audit bundle here, or click to browse
audit_bundle_import.modal_copy_existing_pre: This bundle was already imported on
audit_bundle_import.modal_copy_existing_mid: . Proceeding will re-run the merge — most app data will be unchanged, but any new annotations from
audit_bundle_import.modal_copy_existing_post: will be added with fresh timestamps.
audit_bundle_import.modal_copy_new: {apps, plural, one {About to import # app and} other {About to import # apps and}} {notes, plural, one {# note.} other {# notes.}} Apps you already track will only update if the bundle has newer data; the recommender's notes attach as separate entries alongside any of your own.
audit_bundle_import.result_summary_apps_added: {count, plural, one {Imported # new app} other {Imported # new apps}}
audit_bundle_import.result_summary_notes: {count, plural, one { · # note from {name}} other { · # notes from {name}}}
audit_bundle_import.result_profile_stashed: {name}'s privacy profile is saved as a suggestion you can review under Privacy Profile.

audit_bundle_export.panel_description: Your audit bundle includes the apps you're tracking, their privacy labels, your AI summaries, and any annotations you've written. Private notes are never included.
audit_bundle_export.recommender_hint: Used in the import banner the recipient sees. Falls back to {fallback} if blank.
audit_bundle_export.include_profile_hint: Lets the recipient see why you flagged certain apps. Uncheck to keep your profile private.

queue_banner.title: {count, plural, one {# app waiting to import} other {# apps waiting to import}}
queue_banner.drain_running: Working through the backlog now — keep this tab open or hit Cancel to stop.

filter_banner.hidden: Imports without any matching items are hidden.
filter_banner.retry_all_title: Re-scrape every matching item that has an App Store URL on file

import_history.empty_no_imports: No imports yet. When you add apps via onboarding, each batch will appear here so you can review or revert it.
import_history.stat_queued_title: Queued for background import (Apple rate-limited) — click to filter
import_history.stat_unmatched_title: Apps we couldn't match to the App Store — click to filter
import_history.stat_removed_title: Imported then removed from tracking — click to filter

actions.resume_matching_title: Continue matching the apps Apple rate-limited us on

import_history.empty_no_match_filter: No items in this import match the current filter.

item.removed_note: This app was removed from your dashboard. Background sync will not re-add it.

change_match.title_readd: Search for an app to re-add
change_match.empty: No App Store matches for that name. Try adjusting spelling or include the developer.

legacy.title: No per-app history for this import
legacy.body: This batch was imported before per-app history was recorded. The apps themselves are still in your dashboard — there's just no list to review from here. Future imports will show every app individually.

couldnt_load.title: Couldn't load the app list
couldnt_load.body: {count, plural, one {The server reported # app for this import, but nothing came back. Try again.} other {The server reported # apps for this import, but nothing came back. Try again.}}

policy_alerts.field_help: Default 90. Set to 0 to disable the banner entirely. Changes still appear on the History tab regardless.

policy_throttle.cooldown_help: Default 60. Set to 0 to allow every Re-sync through without waiting. Max 10 080 (one week).
policy_throttle.scrape_disabled_help: When on, privacytracker never fetches privacy policies in the background — no per-app auto-fetch after an App Store sync, no bulk runs, no scheduled refreshes. The manual Re-scrape buttons return an error too. Existing cached policies and their AI summaries stay intact, and you can still re-summarise from cached text. Use this to completely stop background policy activity.
policy_throttle.throttle_inert_when_disabled: Throttle has no effect while policy scraping is disabled above.

privacy_profile_card.hint_on: On — apps are compared to your preferences and mismatches are flagged.
privacy_profile_card.hint_off: Off — apps are shown as-is with no comparison.
privacy_profile_card.saved_count: {count, plural, one {✓ Saved · # category compared} other {✓ Saved · # categories compared}}
privacy_profile_card.unsaved_no_profile: No profile set — apps are shown without comparison.
privacy_profile_card.empty_warn: Pick a tier for at least one category, or turn the toggle off.

accessibility_profile_card.hint_on: On — apps are compared to your accessibility needs and mismatches are flagged.
accessibility_profile_card.hint_off: Off — apps are shown as-is with no accessibility comparison.
accessibility_profile_card.saved_count: {count, plural, one {✓ Saved · # feature compared} other {✓ Saved · # features compared}}
accessibility_profile_card.unsaved_no_profile: No profile set — apps are shown without accessibility comparison.
accessibility_profile_card.empty_warn: Pick a preference for at least one feature, or turn the toggle off.

restore_backup.title: Replace all data with this backup?
restore_backup.no_filename: {count, plural, one {# row across {tables} tables.} other {# rows across {tables} tables.}}
restore_backup.warning_body: Every tracked app, privacy snapshot, policy summary, import record, and setting will be deleted and rebuilt from the backup.

delete_import.title: Remove this import?
delete_import.meta: {date} · {total, plural, one {# app} other {# apps}} · {imported} imported
delete_import.option_history_only_desc: Removes the record of this import. Apps imported from it stay in your dashboard.
delete_import.option_with_apps_label: Delete history and remove imported apps
delete_import.option_with_apps_desc: {count, plural, one {Full revert. # app will be deleted from your dashboard along with their privacy snapshots.} other {Full revert. # apps will be deleted from your dashboard along with their privacy snapshots.}}

remove_app.title: Remove {name} from your dashboard?
remove_app.body: The app will be deleted and the import history will remember it was removed, so future syncs won't silently re-add it. You can always import it again later.

accessibility_labels_card.checkbox_lead: Show accessibility labels in the app
accessibility_labels_card.checkbox_help: When on, the app detail page shows a blue chip with the feature list, the Stats page includes an accessibility coverage chart, and the grid has a filter for apps that do/don't declare any features. Turning this off hides those surfaces but does NOT stop data collection — flipping it back on later reveals the history that was captured in the meantime.

review_queue_card.progress_bar_label: Show progress bar in the review queue
review_queue_card.progress_bar_help: Adds a thin progress bar to the carousel header so you can see how many cards you've worked through. Turn it off if you prefer a cleaner header — the numeric counter (e.g. 3 of 12) stays visible either way.

diagnostics_page.title: Diagnostics
diagnostics_page.subtitle: Live runtime metrics for the Node sidecar. Polls every {seconds}s.

toolbar.copy_title: Copy a full diagnostics bundle as JSON to your clipboard, ready to paste into a GitHub issue
toolbar.download_title: Download a full diagnostics bundle as a JSON file
toolbar.clear_title: Reset event-loop histogram, slow-query ring, and error log

banner.event_loop_danger: App is stalling — event-loop p99 {p99} ms.
banner.heap_full: V8 heap {pct}% full — close to OOM.
banner.swapping: OS is swapping heavily (>1k major page faults). The classic beach-ball cause.
banner.disk_critical: Less than 5% free on the data volume ({free} left).
banner.errors_recent: {count, plural, one {# error} other {# errors}} logged in the last 5 minutes.

card_event_loop.title: Event-loop lag
card_event_loop.help: How long the loop is stalled. p99 above 100ms is jank; above 1s is a beach-ball.

card_memory.title: Memory
card_memory.help: RSS = total resident size. External = native allocations (better-sqlite3 lives here).
card_memory.spark_heap: V8 heap utilisation over recent window

card_resource.title: Resource usage
card_resource.help: Major page faults rising fast usually means the OS is swapping — the classic beach-ball cause.

card_database.title: Database
card_database.help: High freelist count + low utilisation means a VACUUM would reclaim space.
card_database.integrity_check_title: Scans every page. Slow on a large DB; run when investigating corruption.

card_disk.title: Disk + data directory
card_disk.help: Free space on the volume holding your data. Below 10% starts to risk SQLite WAL stalls.
card_disk.subtitle_loading: Volume usage and last automated backup.

card_jobs.title: Background jobs
card_jobs.help: Crash-safe bulk runners. Stale means the mutex is held but the queue blob is missing — usually a kill mid-run.

card_rate_limits.title: Apple rate limits
card_rate_limits.help: Hard cooldowns recorded from Apple 429s, plus the local soft token bucket.
card_rate_limits.subtitle_loading: iTunes search + App Store scrape cooldowns.

card_slow_query.title: Slow queries
card_slow_query.help: {total, plural, one {# slow query} other {# slow queries}} since boot. Showing most recent {recent}.
card_slow_query.no_slow: No slow queries recorded — every DB call has finished under the threshold. ✓
card_slow_query.profiling_off: Profiling is off — turn it on to start capturing.

card_error_log.title: Error log
card_error_log.help: Last {count} server-side console.error / console.warn lines. Restart wipes the buffer.

card_flags.title: Feature-flag overrides
card_flags.help: Flags whose current value differs from their hard default — usually the answer to 'why is feature X showing/hiding?'.
card_flags.no_overrides: No active overrides; every flag is at its default.

background_mode_callout.title: Keep privacytracker running in the background
background_mode_callout.body: Set sync frequency, notification channels, and quiet hours in a quick four-step wizard.
background_mode_callout.dismiss_title: Hide this prompt — you can revisit setup from Settings later

background_mode_wizard.title: Keep privacytracker running in the background
background_mode_wizard.submit_error_generic: Couldn't save your choices — try again or open Settings to set them one at a time.

step_sync.title: How often should we sync?
step_sync.body: We check the App Store for label changes, privacy-policy updates, and version bumps. Privacy-policy scrapes piggyback on this cadence.
step_sync.option_daily_hint: Catches almost everything within 24 hours. Easy on Apple's rate limits.
step_sync.option_weekly_hint: Lightweight — best when you mostly track stable, slow-moving apps.
step_sync.option_manual_hint: Nothing runs in the background. You'll trigger syncs yourself from Settings.

step_notifications.title: How should we let you know?
step_notifications.body: In-app bell is always on. Pick any additional channels you want for change summaries.
step_notifications.bell_hint: Already on. Shows unread changes next to the search bar.
step_notifications.native_hint: Shows a system toast and updates the Dock badge when new changes land. Requires the desktop build.
step_notifications.webhook_hint: Paste an incoming-webhook URL from your chat workspace. We POST summaries directly — no email, no SMTP.
step_notifications.test_failure_generic: Webhook test failed — check the URL and try again.

step_defaults.title: Recommended background defaults
step_defaults.body: These are configurable any time from Settings. Toggle off anything you'd rather skip.
step_defaults.autostart_hint: macOS LaunchAgent — the app starts when you sign in.
step_defaults.launch_hidden_hint: Only the menu-bar icon appears at login. Open the dashboard from there when you need it.

step_quiet.title: Quiet hours
step_quiet.body: Optional: pause notifications during a set window. In-app bell rows still get created — they just hold until the window ends.
step_quiet.enable_hint: Notifications between these times are suppressed and delivered when the window ends.
